personality_voice.book_baseline: The books establish Snape as cruel, sarcastic, and coldly calculating. He is described explicitly as Harry's least favourite teacher, 'cruel, sarcastic, and disliked by everybody except the students from his own house.' His speech is characterised by soft menace ('So,' he said softly), theatrical pauses, triumphant sneering, and precise verbal cruelty. He prowls, looms, and glides. He uses silence as a weapon ('Silence!' snapped twice in quick succession). He is petty in victory — smiling when Harry and Ron are in trouble, looking 'as though Christmas had been canceled' when Dumbledore denies him the expulsion he wants. He is also professionally territorial, icily asserting his authority over Lockhart ('I believe I am the Potions master at this school'). His contempt is specific and targeted, not generic.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core personality faithfully. Snape's cold menace is intact: he separates from shadows, raises an eyebrow, speaks with controlled disdain. His pettiness is shown — the 'look of pure venom' at Harry and Ron after Dumbledore overrules him. His professional rivalry with Lockhart is preserved: 'Perhaps it would be prudent to first teach the students to block unfriendly spells' is a line that carries the same icy condescension as the book. His manipulation of the duelling club — pairing Harry with Malfoy, whispering to Malfoy before the duel, smiling with amusement when the snake appears — is directly evidenced. His calculating look at Harry after the Parseltongue incident is preserved verbatim.
personality_voice.difference: The film loses some of the sustained verbal texture. The book's extended interrogation scene after the flying car — where Snape methodically builds his case, uses Ron's stomach rumble against him, and savours each moment — is compressed in the film into a shorter confrontation. The book's Snape gives longer, more elaborate speeches of cruelty; the film's Snape is slightly more economical. The 'Snape prowled through the fumes, making waspish remarks' pattern of classroom behaviour is not shown in the film corpus. The Valentine's Day scene (Snape looking 'as though the first person to ask him for a Love Potion would be force-fed poison') is absent from the film corpus, removing one texture beat.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality — cold menace, sarcasm, pettiness, professional pride, calculated cruelty — is preserved with high fidelity. The compression loses some of the sustained verbal texture and a few secondary beats, but does not change the character's dominant traits or emotional tone. Minor penalty for loss of texture.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: In CoS, Snape serves several narrative functions: disciplinary antagonist (the flying car confrontation), institutional obstacle (trying to get Harry expelled or punished at multiple points), professional rival to Lockhart (the duelling club), and a figure of ambient menace who stands guard, assigns excessive homework, and keeps pressure on Harry throughout. He also functions as a plot-relevant observer — his calculating look after the Parseltongue incident signals that he is watching and assessing Harry. He initiates confrontations, proposes punishments, and actively tries to influence outcomes (pushing for expulsion, suggesting Quidditch ban, pairing Harry with Malfoy).
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves Snape's role as disciplinary antagonist and institutional obstacle in the flying car scene. His role in the duelling club is intact — he disarms Lockhart, pairs Harry with Malfoy, whispers to Malfoy, and ends the duel with 'Finite Incantatem.' His calculating observation of Harry after the Parseltongue incident is preserved. His confrontation of Lockhart at the staff meeting ('Just the man. A girl has been snatched by the monster, Lockhart. Your moment has come at last') is present and gives him active agency in exposing Lockhart.
narrative_role_agency.difference: Several smaller agency moments are absent from the film corpus: Snape standing guard during the castle lockdown (book scene 139), Snape assigning excessive homework as ongoing pressure (book scene 123), Snape's note giving Slytherin the Quidditch pitch (present in both but Snape himself does not appear in the film scene — it is read aloud by Wood). The book's sustained pattern of Snape as ambient institutional pressure is reduced. However, the key active moments are preserved.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The main narrative functions are intact. Some ambient pressure and minor agency moments are lost, but no major action is transferred to another character. The Quidditch note scene is a minor reduction — Snape's agency is referenced but he does not appear. Moderate penalty for reduced ambient presence.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: The corpus covers only CoS, where Snape's motivations are surface-level: institutional authority, personal dislike of Harry, professional rivalry with Lockhart, and house loyalty (explicitly stating he would expel Harry and Ron if they were in Slytherin). There is no internal conflict visible in this corpus — his double-agent status and love for Lily are not present in CoS. His motivations here are petty and institutional rather than complex.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves the same surface motivations: dislike of Harry, institutional authority, house loyalty, professional rivalry. No internal conflict is added or removed because none exists in this portion of the story.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: No meaningful difference within the scope of this corpus. The motivations are simple and consistently rendered in both versions.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: No penalty warranted within this corpus. The deeper motivations (Lily, double agent) are not part of CoS and cannot be evaluated here. Confidence is low because the corpus does not allow evaluation of Snape's full motivational complexity.

character_arc.book_baseline: Within CoS specifically, Snape has no arc — he is a flat antagonist throughout. He begins as a hostile institutional figure and ends as one. His arc across the full series is profound, but within this single book he does not change. A flat arc here is intentional and correct.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film also presents Snape as flat within CoS. No arc is invented or removed.
character_arc.difference: No difference. Both book and film present a flat Snape in CoS.
character_arc.penalty_logic: Per the scoring rules, a character scores full points if their arc is flat in the books and flat in the films. However, confidence is low because this corpus covers only one book and cannot assess the broader arc fidelity across the series.

key_relationships.book_baseline: The corpus shows three key relationships: (1) Snape/Harry — adversarial, with Snape as the institutional bully who targets Harry specifically and personally; (2) Snape/Dumbledore — Snape defers to Dumbledore but with visible frustration and resentment when overruled; (3) Snape/Lockhart — professional contempt and rivalry, with Snape barely concealing his disdain. The Snape/Malfoy relationship is also present — Snape favours Malfoy, whispers to him, and uses him as a weapon against Harry.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: Snape/Harry adversarial dynamic is preserved. Snape/Dumbledore deference with frustration is shown (Snape tries to push for expulsion, Dumbledore overrules him, Snape casts 'a look of pure venom'). Snape/Lockhart rivalry is preserved through the duelling club. Snape/Malfoy favouritism is shown (whispering to Malfoy, suggesting him as Harry's opponent).
key_relationships.difference: The sustained texture of the Snape/Harry relationship — the ongoing classroom harassment, the excessive homework, the ambient hostility — is reduced in the film. The book builds this relationship through repeated small interactions; the film has fewer of these. The Snape/Dumbledore dynamic loses the 'swept out of his office' moment's full humiliation beat, though the venom look is preserved.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The core dynamics are preserved but the relational texture is thinner in the film. The Snape/Harry relationship in particular loses the sustained classroom pattern that defines its power imbalance. Moderate penalty.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: The book's Snape is more textured than the film's in several ways: (1) the extended interrogation scene after the flying car shows his methodical cruelty and his ability to use information strategically; (2) the classroom scenes show his sustained pattern of bullying Gryffindors while protecting Slytherins; (3) the Valentine's Day reaction adds a moment of dark comedy; (4) his standing guard during the lockdown shows him as a figure of ambient institutional menace; (5) his reaction to Dumbledore's suspension ('Snape gripped the back of a chair very hard') shows a rare moment of genuine alarm that complicates his usual composure.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the most visible complexity beats: the calculating look after Parseltongue, the professional rivalry with Lockhart, the pettiness when overruled. It loses the sustained classroom texture, the Valentine's Day beat, the lockdown guard scene, and the chair-gripping moment of alarm.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The film's Snape is recognisable and faithful in his major appearances but loses the repeated small-scale cruelty and the ambient menace that builds his characterisation across the book. The chair-gripping moment — a rare crack in his composure — is absent. The classroom prowling pattern is absent.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The lost material reduces the texture of Snape's characterisation without fundamentally changing who he is. He remains recognisable and his major traits are intact. The losses are real but not transformative — they simplify rather than distort. Moderate penalty.

lost_or_transferred_material: Extended interrogation scene after flying car — Snape's methodical verbal cruelty and use of Ron's stomach rumble is compressed
Sustained classroom harassment pattern ('prowled through the fumes, making waspish remarks')
Valentine's Day reaction scene (Snape looking as though the first person to ask for a Love Potion would be force-fed poison)
Snape standing guard during castle lockdown (book scene 139)
Snape gripping the back of a chair when told a student has been taken — rare crack in composure
Snape assigning excessive homework as ongoing ambient pressure (book scene 123)
Snape's triumphant smile when Ron's stomach rumbles during interrogation — specific beat of petty cruelty
Snape's 'look of pure venom' when swept out of his own office by Dumbledore — present in film but the full humiliation of being physically removed from his own space is reduced
key_observations: The film's Snape is a faithful but slightly simplified version of the book's. The core personality — cold menace, sarcasm, pettiness, professional pride, calculated cruelty — is preserved with high fidelity, and the major active scenes (flying car confrontation, duelling club, Lockhart confrontation at the staff meeting) are rendered faithfully. The losses are primarily in texture: the sustained classroom harassment pattern, the ambient menace of repeated small interactions, and a few secondary character beats that build complexity without changing the fundamental characterisation. No major action or agency is transferred to another character. The score reflects a faithful but compressed portrayal — recognisable and largely correct, but missing the accumulated detail that makes the book's Snape feel more fully inhabited. Confidence is medium overall because the corpus covers only CoS, preventing evaluation of the deeper motivational and arc dimensions that define Snape as a character across the full series.
